prompt:
Scene:
A square reconstruction image that feels evidence-based, spatially coherent, and controlled enough for analytical review. Build the frame around a complete spatial read of the reconstructed environment. Keep the lighting, camera distance, and spatial organization tuned for a high-signal research image rather than a casual snapshot. The image belongs to the Forensic Scene Reconstruction theme and should feel immediately legible as a prompt atlas theme for forensic scene reconstruction, testing physics-materials, reasoning-composition, style-transfer with gpt image 2..

Subject:
Center the study on a complete spatial read of the reconstructed environment. Translate the theme into a concrete visual statement instead of an abstract mood board: the viewer should understand what kind of image this is, what is being tested, and why the composition is difficult. Use the slug idea of forensic scene reconstruction as a literal guide for subject matter, materials, and staging.

Important details:
The image should communicate inference and real-world knowledge through placement, materials, object relationships, and disciplined visual hierarchy. Preserve a disciplined square composition, clear focal hierarchy, and enough secondary information to make the image feel complete rather than underspecified. The image should look polished, novel, and exhibition-ready, with topic-specific credibility coming from the way surfaces, structure, and visual language reinforce Forensic Scene Reconstruction. Avoid generic filler elements that do not serve the main concept.

Use case:
Forensic Scene Reconstruction single study 1/8, testing physics-materials, reasoning-composition, style-transfer through the scene overview variation.

Constraints:
No readable text, no watermark, no logos, preserve exact 1:1 aspect ratio, no readable evidence tags, no police logos, no gore, no sensational crime-drama theatrics, no broken spatial logic, no arbitrary clutter.
title: Scene Overview
description: Scene Overview interprets Forensic Scene Reconstruction as a self-contained image study with a distinct visual emphasis.
